title: Data Structures
description: Core Python data structures: Lists, Dictionaries, Tuples, and Sets.

tutorial.title: What is a List?
tutorial.content:
A list is an ordered collection of items. You can put anything in a list:
numbers, strings, booleans, even other lists!

Lists are MUTABLE, meaning you can change them after creation.
This makes them one of the most versatile tools in Python.

tutorial.title: List Operations
tutorial.content:
fruits = ['apple', 'banana']
fruits.append('cherry')     # Add to end
print(fruits[0])             # Access first item → 'apple'
print(len(fruits))           # Length → 3

tutorial.title: Common List Methods
tutorial.content:
.append(x)    Add x to the end
.insert(i, x) Insert x at position i
.remove(x)    Remove first occurrence of x
.pop()        Remove & return last item
.sort()       Sort the list in place
.reverse()    Reverse the list in place
.index(x)     Return index of first x
len(list)     Get the length

tutorial.title: Pro Tip: Slicing
tutorial.content:
Access multiple items at once with slicing:

  nums = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
  nums[0:3]    →  [0, 1, 2]      (items at 0, 1, 2)
  nums[2:]     →  [2, 3, 4, 5]   (from index 2 to end)
  nums[:3]     →  [0, 1, 2]      (first 3 items)
  nums[-1]     →  5              (last item)
  nums[::-1]   →  [5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0]  (reversed!)

tutorial.title: Try It: List Slicing
tutorial.content:
nums = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(nums[1:4])    # [1, 2, 3]
print(nums[::-1])   # Reversed!
print(nums[-2:])    # Last 2 items

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: Which method adds an item to the END of a list?

tutorial.title: Key-Value Pairs
tutorial.content:
Dictionaries store data in key-value pairs, like a real dictionary
(Word → Definition). They are optimized for fast lookups.

Use a dictionary when you have labeled data:
  ages = {'Alice': 30, 'Bob': 25}
  print(ages['Alice'])  →  30

tutorial.title: Dictionary Example
tutorial.content:
ages = {'Alice': 30, 'Bob': 25}
print(ages['Alice'])          # 30
ages['Charlie'] = 35          # Add new key
print(ages)

tutorial.title: Safe Lookup with .get()
tutorial.content:
Accessing a key that doesn't exist causes a KeyError.
Use .get() to avoid crashes:

  ages = {'Alice': 30}
  print(ages['Bob'])         # KeyError!
  print(ages.get('Bob'))     # None (safe)
  print(ages.get('Bob', 0)) # 0   (with default value)

tutorial.title: Iterating Over Dictionaries
tutorial.content:
Three useful methods for iteration:

  d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2}

  d.keys()    →  dict_keys(['a', 'b'])
  d.values()  →  dict_values([1, 2])
  d.items()   →  dict_items([('a',1), ('b',2)])

Most common: loop over .items() to get key and value together:
  for key, value in d.items():
      print(f'{key} = {value}')

tutorial.title: Try It: Dict Methods
Quick Check
tutorial.question: What does `d.get('missing_key', 'default')` return if the key doesn't exist?

tutorial.title: Sets vs Tuples
tutorial.content:
SETS are unordered collections of UNIQUE items (no duplicates).
  → Use when you care about membership, not order.
  → Created with curly braces: {1, 2, 3}

TUPLES are ordered collections that CANNOT be changed (immutable).
  → Use for fixed data that shouldn't change.
  → Created with parentheses: (10, 20)

tutorial.title: Set & Tuple Example
tutorial.content:
unique_nums = {1, 2, 2, 3, 3}  # Duplicates removed!
print(unique_nums)             # {1, 2, 3}

point = (10, 20)               # Immutable!
print(point[0])                # 10

tutorial.title: Set Operations (Like Math!)
tutorial.content:
Sets support mathematical operations:

  a = {1, 2, 3, 4}
  b = {3, 4, 5, 6}

  a | b   →  {1,2,3,4,5,6}   (Union: all items)
  a & b   →  {3, 4}          (Intersection: common items)
  a - b   →  {1, 2}          (Difference: in a but not b)

Useful for: finding unique visitors, common friends, etc.

tutorial.title: Try It: Set Operations
Quick Check
tutorial.question: What does `{1, 2, 2, 3}` evaluate to in Python?

tutorial.title: Pythonic Loops
tutorial.content:
List comprehension is a concise way to create lists.
It replaces simple 'for' loops with a single line of code.

  Traditional:
    squares = []
    for x in range(5):
        squares.append(x*x)

  Comprehension:
    squares = [x*x for x in range(5)]

Same result, more Pythonic!

tutorial.title: Comprehension Example
tutorial.content:
squares = [x*x for x in range(5)]
print(squares)  # [0, 1, 4, 9, 16]

# With a filter condition:
evens = [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]
print(evens)    # [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]

tutorial.title: Dict Comprehensions Too!
Quick Check
tutorial.question: What does `[x*2 for x in range(3)]` produce?

tutorial.title: LIFO and FIFO
tutorial.content:
Stacks (LIFO: Last-In, First-Out) and Queues (FIFO: First-In, First-Out)
are fundamental data structures.

  Stack  → Like a stack of plates. You add/remove from the TOP.
  Queue  → Like a line at a store. First in, first served.

tutorial.title: Stack with a List
tutorial.content:
stack = []
stack.append('A')   # Push
stack.append('B')
stack.append('C')
print(stack.pop())  # Pop → 'C' (last in, first out)
print(stack)

tutorial.title: Queue with collections.deque
tutorial.content:
For queues, use collections.deque — it's more efficient than a list
for adding/removing from the left:

  from collections import deque
  q = deque()
  q.append('A')       # Enqueue
  q.append('B')
  q.popleft()         # Dequeue → 'A' (first in, first out)

Real uses: browser history (stack), printer queue (queue)

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: In a STACK, which item is removed first?

tutorial.title: Manipulating Text
tutorial.content:
Strings in Python come with many useful methods for searching,
splitting, joining, and transforming text.

Strings are IMMUTABLE — methods return a new string, they don't
change the original.

tutorial.title: Common String Methods
tutorial.content:
s = '  hello world  '
print(s.upper())          # '  HELLO WORLD  '
print(s.strip())          # 'hello world'  (removes whitespace)
print(s.strip().title())  # 'Hello World'

tutorial.title: More Essential Methods
tutorial.content:
s = 'hello world'

.split()           →  ['hello', 'world']
.split(',')        →  split by comma
.replace('o','0')  →  'hell0 w0rld'
.startswith('he')  →  True
.endswith('ld')    →  True
.find('world')     →  6  (index of match)
'_'.join(['a','b','c'])  →  'a_b_c'

tutorial.title: Try It: String Processing
tutorial.content:
sentence = 'Python is awesome for AI!'
words = sentence.lower().split()
print(words)
print('Count of words:', len(words))
print('Joined:', '-'.join(words))

tutorial.title: Data within Data
tutorial.content:
You can nest lists inside lists (like a 2D matrix) or dictionaries
inside dictionaries. This is very common for representing complex,
hierarchical data — like JSON from a web API.

tutorial.title: Accessing Nested Data
tutorial.content:
matrix = [[1, 2, 3],
          [4, 5, 6],
          [7, 8, 9]]

print(matrix[0])     # [1, 2, 3]  (first row)
print(matrix[1][2])  # 6          (row 1, col 2)

tutorial.title: Nested Dictionaries (Real-World!)
